title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Living Virtually: Privacy in the Metaverse

texts.A: The transition from a 2D internet to an immersive 'Metaverse' is a privacy nightmare of unprecedented proportions. The architecture of these virtual worlds is being deliberately designed to maximize engagement and data extraction, not to protect the fundamental rights of their users—a fact that the industry's euphemistic language of 'seamless experiences' is calculated to obscure. In a virtual world, companies aren't just tracking your clicks; they are tracking your eye movements, your gait, and even your physiological responses to stimuli. [37]While some argue that this data is necessary for a 'seamless' experience, I believe it represents the final frontier of surveillance capitalism.[/37] [39]If we don't establish 'human-centric' digital rights now, we will find ourselves living in a world where our very thoughts are being harvested for advertising.[/39] The surveillance architecture of the Metaverse will be as invisible as it is pervasive, embedded in every interaction, every expression, and every unconscious hesitation.
texts.B: The Metaverse offers an incredible opportunity to redefine human connection, transcending physical and geographical boundaries. For people with disabilities, those living in remote communities, or individuals navigating social anxiety, immersive virtual spaces could be profoundly transformative in ways that the flat, asynchronous internet never managed to achieve. [38]I disagree with those who believe that the privacy risks are fundamentally different from those of current social media; we already give away vast amounts of data, and the Metaverse is simply an evolution of that trend.[/38] [40]By using blockchain and decentralized identity protocols, we can actually give users more control over their digital personas than they have in the real world.[/40] We should focus on the creative potential of these spaces rather than being paralyzed by fear. Constructive engagement with the technology is the only way to shape it toward equitable outcomes; alarmist rhetoric serves neither users nor developers. The history of communications technology suggests that concerns about new platforms, though valid, rarely prove as catastrophic as initially feared when adequate governance structures are permitted to develop alongside the technology.
texts.C: The real danger of the Metaverse is the potential for 'behavioral engineering.' If a company can control the environment you inhabit, they can subtly nudge your decisions in ways that are invisible to you. The architecture of these spaces is not neutral; it is purposefully engineered to maximize commercial outcomes by exploiting the cognitive vulnerabilities of users who are entirely unaware they are being manipulated. [37]I share the view that the level of biometric data collection required for VR is inherently invasive and cannot be 'anonymized' in any meaningful way.[/37] [38]Unlike those who see this as a 'simple evolution,' I believe the immersive nature of the Metaverse makes the psychological impact of data-mining far more profound.[/38] We are not just users of the Metaverse; we are the product. Without independent oversight and enforceable privacy standards, this technology represents an unprecedented transfer of power from citizens to corporations that no subsequent regulation will be able to fully reverse.
texts.D: As with any new technology, the initial reaction to the Metaverse is one of alarmism. Every major communications innovation—from the printing press to television to social media—has been greeted with similar warnings of social collapse that have, in retrospect, proved to be significantly overstated. [40]I share the view that decentralized technologies will eventually solve the trust issues that plague our current centralized platforms.[/40] However, we must be realistic; most users are willing to trade a degree of privacy for a superior, more entertaining experience. [39]I am less convinced than some of my colleagues that 'digital rights' legislation can keep pace with the speed of innovation.[/39] The market, not the government, will ultimately decide which platforms succeed based on how well they protect their users' interests. Users who feel surveilled and manipulated will naturally gravitate toward privacy-respecting alternatives, making competition a more reliable driver of good behaviour than legislation. In an open marketplace, privacy-conscious design will ultimately prove more commercially durable than exploitative surveillance-based business models that erode the user trust on which long-term platform success depends.

questions.question: Which expert has a similar view to A on the invasive nature of the data collected in the Metaverse?
questions.explanation: A describes the tracking of eye movements and physiology as a 'nightmare.' C shares this, stating that biometric data collection is 'inherently invasive' and cannot be anonymized.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding whether the Metaverse represents a significantly new type of privacy threat?
questions.explanation: B believes the privacy risks are NOT fundamentally different from current social media. C disagrees, stating that 'unlike those who see this as a simple evolution,' the immersive nature makes the impact 'far more profound.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the likelihood of using legislation to protect users' digital rights?
questions.explanation: A insists we MUST establish digital rights now to prevent disaster. D is 'less convinced' that such legislation 'can keep pace with the speed of innovation,' preferring to trust the market.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's optimism about the role of decentralized technology in protecting privacy?
questions.explanation: B suggests that 'blockchain and decentralized identity' will give users more control. D also 'shares the view that decentralized technologies will eventually solve the trust issues.'
